grade_mapping: Approximate mapping: age 8 = UK Year 3 / US Grade 3, age 9 = UK Year 4 / US Grade 4, age 10 = UK Year 5 / US Grade 5, age 11 = UK Year 6 / US Grade 6, age 12 = UK Year 7 / US Grade 7, age 13 = UK Year 8 / US Grade 8, age 14 = UK Year 9 / US Grade 9, age 15 = UK Year 10 / US Grade 10.

subjects.name: Math
subjects.summary: Consolidated from UK National Curriculum maths, Common Core maths, Khan Academy course sequencing and 11+ maths/reasoning preparation.

topics.title: Number and place value
topics.description: Read, write, order, compare, round and reason with whole numbers, decimals and negative numbers.
topics.alignment: UK: number and place value across KS2 and KS3. US: Common Core Number and Operations in Base Ten and Number System.

samples.question: What is the value of the digit 6 in 462?
Round 48,763 to the nearest 1,000.
Write 6,304,012 in expanded form.
Put these in order: -2, 0.4, -0.5, 1/3.
Write 320,000 as 3.2 x 10^n. What is n?
Write 0.00072 in standard form.
A distance is 4.6 x 10^8 m. Write it as an ordinary number and in kilometres.
A value rounded to 3 significant figures is 0.00497. Give the upper and lower bounds.

topics.title: Four operations
topics.description: Fluent mental and written methods for addition, subtraction, multiplication and division, including order of operations.
topics.alignment: UK: KS2 written and mental calculation, KS3 calculation strategies. US: Common Core operations and rational-number operations.

samples.question: Work out 346 + 278.
Work out 38 x 24.
Work out 4,368 divided by 12.
Evaluate 18 - 3 x (4 - 9).
Evaluate -18 + 6 x (4 - 9) and explain the order of operations used.
Work out (3/4 - 2/5) divided by 7/10. Give your answer in simplest form.
Simplify 3sqrt(12) - sqrt(27) + 2sqrt(3).

topics.title: Fractions equivalence and operations
topics.description: Understand fractions as numbers, compare and simplify them, then add, subtract, multiply and divide fractions.
topics.alignment: UK: KS2 fractions and KS3 rational-number work. US: Common Core Number and Operations - Fractions and rational numbers.

samples.question: Which is larger: 1/2 or 1/4?
Complete: 3/4 = ?/20.
Work out 2/3 + 1/6.
Work out 5/6 - 1/4.
Work out 2/3 x 3/5, then simplify.
Work out 5/6 divided by 2/3.
Simplify (x^2 - 9) / (x + 3), stating any excluded value of x.

topics.title: Decimals and percentages
topics.description: Link fractions, decimals and percentages; compare, round and calculate with them in practical contexts.
topics.alignment: UK: decimals and percentages in upper KS2 and proportional reasoning in KS3. US: decimal fractions, percent and rational-number applications.

samples.question: Write 4 tenths as a decimal.
Which is greater: 0.37 or 0.4?
Find 35% of 80.
Increase 60 by 15%.
Write 0.125 as a fraction and as a percentage.
A price increases by 10% and then decreases by 10%. Is the final price the same as the original? Explain.
A jacket costs GBP 68 after a 15% discount. What was the original price?
GBP 1,200 is invested at 3.5% compound interest for 3 years. Find the final amount to the nearest penny.

topics.title: Ratio and proportion
topics.description: Use multiplicative comparison, equivalent ratios, scale factors, rates and proportional relationships.
topics.alignment: UK: Year 6 ratio/proportion and KS3 rates of change. US: Common Core Ratios and Proportional Relationships.

samples.question: There are 2 red counters for every 3 blue counters. How many counters are there if there are 6 red counters?
A recipe uses 3 eggs for 12 cakes. How many eggs for 24 cakes?
Split 30 sweets in the ratio 2:3.
A map scale is 1 cm to 5 km. What distance is 7 cm?
A graph shows y = 4x. What is y when x = 7, and what is the constant of proportionality?
y is directly proportional to x. When x = 6, y = 42. Find y when x = 11.
y is inversely proportional to x. When x = 4, y = 18. Find y when x = 9.
Two similar solids have side lengths in the ratio 2:5. Their smaller volume is 64 cm^3. Find the larger volume.

topics.title: Algebraic thinking and equations
topics.description: Move from missing-number problems to expressions, formulae, equations, simplification and pattern generalisation.
topics.alignment: UK: Year 6 algebra and KS3 algebra. US: Common Core patterns, expressions and equations.

samples.question: 7 + ? = 19. What is the missing number?
3 x n = 27. What is n?
Evaluate 4a + 3 when a = 5.
Solve 2x + 5 = 17.
Simplify 3(2x - 1) + x.
Expand and simplify 4(2x - 3) - 2(x + 5).
Solve the simultaneous equations: 2x + y = 11 and x - y = 1.
Solve x^2 - 5x - 24 = 0.

topics.title: Geometry, angles and properties of shapes
topics.description: Recognise, classify, draw and reason about 2-D and 3-D shapes, angles, symmetry and circles.
topics.alignment: UK: geometry across KS2 and KS3. US: Common Core Geometry.

samples.question: How many right angles does a rectangle have?
Name a quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides.
Two angles on a straight line are 65 degrees and x. What is x?
A triangle has angles 48 and 72 degrees. Find the third angle.
A circle has radius 5 cm. What is its diameter?
Find the interior angle of a regular 12-sided polygon.
A right triangle has shorter sides 9 cm and 12 cm. Find the hypotenuse.
A ladder 5 m long leans against a wall at 68 degrees to the ground. How high up the wall does it reach?

topics.title: Statistics and probability
topics.description: Represent, interpret and compare data; understand averages, probability models and chance.
topics.alignment: UK: statistics across KS2 and KS3. US: Common Core Measurement/Data and Statistics/Probability.

samples.question: A bar chart shows 8 apples and 5 bananas. How many more apples?
A line graph rises from 12 to 20. What is the increase?
Find the mean of 4, 7, 9, 10.
In a pie chart of 40 pupils, 10 choose football. What angle should football be?
A bag has 3 red and 5 blue counters. What is P(red)?
A fair spinner has sectors 1, 2, 3 and 4. It is spun twice. What is the probability of getting a total of 7?
Estimate the mean from a grouped frequency table with intervals 0-10, 10-20, 20-30.
A medical test is 95% accurate for people with a condition and 90% accurate for people without it. What extra information is needed to find P(condition | positive test)?

subjects.name: English Language
subjects.summary: Consolidated from UK National Curriculum English, Common Core ELA and Khan Academy ELA/grammar coverage.

topics.title: Reading fluency and comprehension
topics.description: Read age-appropriate fiction, poetry, drama and nonfiction with accuracy, stamina and literal understanding.
topics.alignment: UK: KS2 reading and KS3 increasingly challenging texts. US: Common Core Reading Literature and Informational Text.

samples.question: Read a short fable. What problem does the main character face?
Read a nonfiction paragraph. Which sentence states the main idea?
Read two paragraphs. Summarise the key point in one sentence.
Read an article extract. Which evidence best supports the author's claim?
Read a short essay. How does the structure help the argument develop?
Read a persuasive article. How does the writer guide the reader from problem to solution?
Evaluate how the writer uses structure to build tension across the extract.
Compare how two nonfiction texts present different attitudes to technology.

topics.title: Inference and evidence
topics.description: Infer feelings, motives, viewpoints and implied meaning, then justify answers with textual evidence.
topics.alignment: UK: KS2 inference and justification; KS3 inference and evidence. US: Common Core inference, evidence and analysis.

samples.question: Mia slammed the door and wiped her eyes. How is Mia feeling? What clue tells you?
A character says 'Fine' but avoids eye contact. What might they really mean?
Find two details that show the narrator is nervous.
Which quotation best supports the idea that the speaker is unreliable?
Explain how a writer implies a character's motive without stating it directly.
A narrator praises a character but describes their actions as selfish. What can you infer about the narrator?
Identify two details that imply a speaker is biased, and explain their effect.
Synthesize evidence from two paragraphs to infer the writer's unstated criticism.

topics.title: Vocabulary and context clues
topics.description: Use context, morphology, word roots and reference skills to clarify word meanings and nuance.
topics.alignment: UK: vocabulary, morphology, dictionaries and thesaurus use. US: Common Core Language vocabulary acquisition.

samples.question: In 'The path was narrow', what does narrow mean?
Use context to choose the meaning of 'bark': The tree's bark was rough.
What does the prefix 'mis-' mean in 'misjudge'?
Choose the best synonym for 'reluctant' in this sentence.
Explain the difference between 'assert', 'argue' and 'suggest'.
Use context and word roots to infer the meaning of "benevolent" in a sentence.
Explain how the words "slithered", "hissed" and "coiled" create a semantic field.
Choose the most precise academic word to replace "shows" in an analytical paragraph, and justify your choice.

topics.title: Grammar and sentence structure
topics.description: Understand parts of speech, clauses, phrases, tense, agreement and sentence variety.
topics.alignment: UK: grammar appendix and KS2-KS3 grammar/vocabulary. US: Common Core Language conventions.

samples.question: Identify the verb in: The dog chased the ball.
Add an adjective to improve: The house stood on the hill.
Identify the subordinate clause: Although it rained, we played.
Rewrite using the passive voice: The team scored three goals.
Combine two clauses using a semicolon correctly.
Rewrite a simple sentence as a complex sentence using a subordinate clause at the start.
Explain how a series of short sentences changes the pace of a narrative.
Analyse how sentence structure supports the writer's argument in a short persuasive paragraph.

topics.title: Punctuation, spelling and mechanics
topics.description: Apply punctuation, capitalisation, spelling patterns and proofreading conventions accurately.
topics.alignment: UK: spelling, punctuation and grammar. US: Common Core Language conventions.

samples.question: Add capital letters and full stops: today we visited london
Choose the correct homophone: Their/There/They're coats are wet.
Add commas to separate items in a list.
Add punctuation for direct speech.
Correct the punctuation and agreement errors in a complex sentence.
Punctuate a sentence containing embedded clauses and direct speech.
Use a colon, semicolon and dash correctly in three linked sentences.
Edit a paragraph for formal academic tone, punctuation accuracy and sentence control.

topics.title: Narrative writing
topics.description: Plan, draft and revise stories with setting, character, atmosphere, dialogue and coherent plot.
topics.alignment: UK: KS2-KS3 writing composition. US: Common Core narrative writing standards.

samples.question: Write three sentences opening a story in a forest.
Write a paragraph that introduces a character through action.
Write a short scene using dialogue and description.
Write a story opening that creates suspense without saying 'scary'.
Write a scene that shifts pace from calm to urgent.
Write a scene from two viewpoints, showing how each character interprets the same event differently.
Write an opening that uses setting, motif and delayed information to create intrigue.
Write a concise exam-style narrative opening that establishes voice, conflict and atmosphere in 250 words.

topics.title: Opinion and argument writing
topics.description: Develop opinions and arguments using claims, reasons, evidence, counterpoints and appropriate tone.
topics.alignment: UK: reasoned justification and arguments/debates. US: Common Core opinion and argument writing.

samples.question: Give one reason why playtime should be longer.
Write a short opinion paragraph with two reasons.
Write a claim and evidence about whether homework helps.
Write a balanced paragraph including a counterargument.
Evaluate which of two pieces of evidence better supports a claim.
Write a paragraph with a claim, evidence, explanation and a counterpoint.
Analyse how rhetorical questions and repetition strengthen a speech.
Write a thesis paragraph that includes a concession and a clear line of argument.

topics.title: Literary analysis and figurative language
topics.description: Discuss themes, conventions, imagery, metaphor, simile, tone, structure and authorial choices.
topics.alignment: UK: upper KS2 and KS3 literary study. US: Common Core literature analysis and language standards.

samples.question: Find a simile in this sentence: The moon was like a coin.
What mood is created by 'grey clouds crowded the sky'?
What theme is suggested by a story about sharing?
Explain how a metaphor changes the reader's view of a character.
Compare how two texts present courage.
Explain how imagery of light and darkness develops a theme in a short extract.
Compare how two poems use natural imagery to present conflict.
Analyse how dramatic irony and structure affect the audience's response in a play extract.

subjects.name: Verbal Reasoning
subjects.summary: A 11+/cognitive-reasoning progression. This is not a statutory UK or US curriculum subject, but it overlaps with vocabulary, grammar, comprehension and logic.

topics.title: Synonyms and antonyms
topics.description: Recognise close and opposite meanings, including precise vocabulary choices under time pressure.
topics.alignment: Related to English vocabulary standards and 11+ verbal reasoning familiarisation.

samples.question: Choose the word closest in meaning to 'happy': joyful, tired, angry, cold.
Choose the opposite of 'ancient': old, modern, stone, early.
Which word is closest to 'cautious': careful, careless, quick, bright?
Choose the best antonym for 'expand': increase, shrink, explain, open.
Which word is nearest in meaning to 'ambiguous': unclear, obvious, loud, brief?
Choose the closest synonym for "meticulous": careless, precise, rapid, generous.
Choose the best antonym for "conciliatory": aggressive, helpful, careful, brief.
Which word is closest in meaning to "equivocal": ambiguous, equal, honest, emotional?

topics.title: Analogies
topics.description: Identify relationships between word pairs and apply the same relationship to a new pair.
topics.alignment: Related to vocabulary, reasoning and common 11+ verbal reasoning item types.

samples.question: Hand is to glove as foot is to what?
Bird is to nest as dog is to what?
Hot is to cold as victory is to what?
Author is to book as composer is to what?
Microscope is to tiny as telescope is to what?
Evaporation is to liquid as sublimation is to what?
Premise is to conclusion as evidence is to what?
Paradox is to contradiction as euphemism is to what relationship?

topics.title: Odd one out and word groups
topics.description: Classify words by meaning, category, function or property and detect the item that does not fit.
topics.alignment: Related to word relationships, vocabulary precision and 11+ verbal reasoning.

samples.question: Which does not belong: red, blue, chair, green?
Which does not belong: salmon, trout, shark, robin?
Which word does not belong: triangle, square, circle, apple?
Which does not belong: sprint, jog, whisper, stroll?
Which does not belong: democracy, monarchy, geology, republic?
Which does not belong: allegory, metaphor, simile, denominator?
Which does not belong: hypothesis, evidence, conclusion, peninsula?
Which does not belong by function: nevertheless, however, therefore, mountain?

topics.title: Letter sequences
topics.description: Identify alphabetical patterns, skips and alternating rules.
topics.alignment: Related to pattern reasoning and common 11+ verbal reasoning sequence tasks.

samples.question: What comes next: A, B, C, ?
What comes next: A, C, E, G, ?
What comes next: Z, X, V, T, ?
What comes next: B, E, D, G, F, I, ?
What comes next: AZ, BY, CX, DW, ?
What comes next: A, D, I, P, ?
What comes next: AZ, CY, EX, GW, ?
What comes next: B, E, J, Q, Z, ?

topics.title: Letter and word codes
topics.description: Use substitution, position and rule-based codes to encode or decode words.
topics.alignment: Related to pattern reasoning, precision and 11+ verbal reasoning codes.

samples.question: If A=1 and B=2, what is C?
If CAT is DBU by moving each letter forward one, write DOG in the same code.
If A=26 and Z=1, what is B?
If CODE becomes DPEF, decode UFTU.
If TRAIN is coded as UQBMJ by alternating +1 and -1, code PLANE.
If SMART becomes TNBSS by alternating +1 and -1 shifts, code LOGIC.
A code reverses the word and then shifts each letter forward two places. Code REASON.
If the code uses letter positions, vowels doubled and consonants reduced by 1, encode PLANE.

topics.title: Missing words and sentence completion
topics.description: Use grammar, syntax and meaning to fill gaps with the most fitting word.
topics.alignment: Related to English grammar, reading comprehension and 11+ verbal skills materials.

samples.question: The cat sat ___ the mat. Choose: on, blue, quickly.
She was tired, ___ she finished the race. Choose: but, because, under.
The scientist recorded the results ___ the experiment ended.
Choose the most precise word: The rain ___ against the window.
Complete the sentence with a word that preserves tone and logic.
Choose the word that best completes the sentence: The argument was persuasive ___ it lacked reliable data.
Complete a paragraph so the tone remains formal and the logical contrast is clear.
Choose the connective that best signals concession rather than cause, contrast or sequence.

topics.title: Verbal deduction
topics.description: Draw conclusions from written clues and constraints.
topics.alignment: Related to reading evidence, mathematical reasoning and 11+ puzzle-style logic.

samples.question: Tom is taller than Sam. Sam is taller than Lee. Who is shortest?
A is before B. C is after B. Which is first?
Three children have red, blue and green bags. Use clues to match each child.
If all glips are flons, and no flons are red, can a glip be red?
Use five clues to order four people by age and justify the order.
Four pupils sit in a row. A is left of B, C is not at an end, and D is right of A. List a possible order.
If all merans are tolins, some tolins are veks, and no veks are blue, what cannot be concluded?
Use six clues to determine a unique order of five competitors and identify the redundant clue.

topics.title: Short-passage verbal reasoning
topics.description: Extract precise meaning from short texts and answer logic or vocabulary questions.
topics.alignment: Related to English comprehension, vocabulary and 11+ English/verbal tests.

samples.question: Read two sentences. What happened first?
Which word in the passage means 'small'?
Which statement must be true based on the paragraph?
Which conclusion is supported, but not directly stated?
Identify an assumption in a short argument.
Identify the assumption behind a short editorial paragraph.
Which sentence most weakens the writer's argument, and why?
Separate fact, inference and opinion in a dense nonfiction extract.

subjects.name: Non Verbal
subjects.summary: A 11+/spatial-reasoning progression. This is not a statutory UK or US curriculum subject, but it overlaps with geometry, transformations and mathematical reasoning.

topics.title: Shape sequences
topics.description: Identify changing visual rules involving shape, size, colour, number, position or orientation.
topics.alignment: Related to geometry, mathematical patterns and 11+ non-verbal reasoning familiarisation.

samples.question: Circle, square, circle, square, ?. What comes next?
Triangle with 1 dot, 2 dots, 3 dots. How many dots next?
A shape rotates a quarter turn each step. What is next?
Two rules change: shape alternates and dots increase by 2. What is next?
Infer the next figure when size, shading and rotation all change.
A sequence changes shape, rotation and shading in a repeating cycle. Identify the next figure.
Find the missing figure when one attribute changes by position and another by number.
Infer the next figure in a sequence with three simultaneous rules and one distractor rule.

topics.title: Odd one out with visual properties
topics.description: Compare figures by attributes and identify the figure that breaks the shared rule.
topics.alignment: Related to geometry classification and common 11+ non-verbal reasoning item types.

samples.question: Which is different: three blue circles and one blue square?
Which is different: three shapes with 4 sides and one with 3 sides?
Which is different: figures with one line of symmetry except one?
Which is different: all rotate clockwise except one rotates anticlockwise?
Identify the figure that breaks a combined rule of shading, rotation and number.
Identify the only figure that does not preserve rotational symmetry after a quarter turn.
Find the odd figure when all others share a hidden relationship between dots, sides and shading.
Choose the odd figure where two visible rules fit but a third structural rule fails.

topics.title: Figure matrices
topics.description: Complete 2x2 or 3x3 grids by applying row and column rules.
topics.alignment: Related to visual logic, mathematical structure and 11+ non-verbal matrices.

samples.question: In a 2x2 grid, the top row is circle then square. Bottom left is circle. What is bottom right?
Rows change from empty to shaded. Columns change from small to large. What belongs in the missing box?
In each row, the third figure combines the first two. Complete the row.
Apply one rule across rows and another down columns in a 3x3 matrix.
Complete a matrix where shape, shading and rotation each follow separate rules.
Complete a 3x3 matrix where each row combines shape and shading from the first two cells.
Complete a matrix where rows control rotation and columns control number of internal marks.
Complete a matrix with independent row, column and diagonal rules.

topics.title: Rotation
topics.description: Mentally rotate figures and recognise equivalent orientations.
topics.alignment: UK: position/direction and transformations. US: geometry and coordinate transformations.

samples.question: Which arrow shows a quarter turn clockwise from up?
A triangle points right. After a half turn, where does it point?
Match a shape to the same shape after a 90-degree rotation.
A shape rotates 90 degrees clockwise each step. Choose the fourth figure.
Distinguish rotation from reflection in a complex figure.
Choose the same figure after a 180-degree rotation when internal arrows also rotate.
Identify whether a figure has been rotated 90 degrees clockwise or reflected vertically.
Track a compound transformation: rotate 90 degrees, reflect in a vertical line, then translate.

topics.title: Reflection and symmetry
topics.description: Recognise mirror images, lines of symmetry and reflected coordinate positions.
topics.alignment: UK: symmetry and reflection. US: geometry and coordinate plane.

samples.question: Which half makes a butterfly symmetrical?
Draw the mirror image of a shape across a vertical line.
How many lines of symmetry does a regular pentagon have?
Reflect point (3,2) in the y-axis.
Choose the image that is a reflection, not a rotation.
Complete the mirror image of a compound figure with asymmetric internal shading.
Choose the result of reflecting a coordinate shape in the line y = x.
Determine whether two figures are congruent by reflection, rotation, translation or no single transformation.

topics.title: Nets and folding
topics.description: Predict 3-D forms from 2-D nets and reason about opposite faces.
topics.alignment: UK: Year 6 nets and KS3 geometry. US: geometry and surface-area reasoning.

samples.question: Which 2-D shape folds into a cube? Choose from simple nets.
A cube net has a star on one face. Which face will touch it?
Which of four nets cannot make a cube?
Find the face opposite the shaded face after folding a cube net.
Use a net with symbols to choose the correct folded cube.
From a cube net with symbols, identify which two symbols will be on opposite faces.
Choose the only cube view that can be made from a given net with arrows on three faces.
Use two partial cube views to infer all possible opposite-face pairs.

topics.title: Counting blocks and embedded shapes
topics.description: Count visible and hidden components, layers, intersections or embedded shapes.
topics.alignment: Related to geometry, area, volume and spatial visualisation.

samples.question: How many squares are in a 2 by 2 grid?
How many small cubes make a 2 by 2 by 2 cube?
Count all triangles in a simple subdivided triangle.
A block stack has hidden cubes. What is the minimum number of cubes?
Find the total number of cubes in a layered 3-D stack from front and side views.
Count all rectangles in a 3 by 4 grid.
Find the minimum number of cubes in a stack shown by front, side and top views.
Count all triangles in a layered geometric figure and explain a systematic counting method.

topics.title: Shape analogies
topics.description: Transfer a visual relationship from one pair of figures to another.
topics.alignment: Related to mathematical structure, analogy and 11+ non-verbal reasoning.

samples.question: Circle becomes square. Triangle becomes what if the rule is 'add one side'?
White shape becomes shaded. Apply the same change to a new shape.
Small dotted triangle becomes large striped triangle. Apply to a circle.
Figure A changes to B by rotation and shading. Apply both to C.
Infer a compound transformation involving shape, number and position.
Figure A changes to B by rotation, enlargement and colour inversion. Apply the same changes to C.
Apply a transformation where outer shape follows one rule and inner marks follow another.
Infer a visual analogy where position, count, shading and symmetry all change together.
